char * strcat(char *dest, const String &.brief: Concatenates a String object to a C-style string buffer.
char * strcat(char *dest, const String &.details: This function appends the contents of a String object to the end of a null-terminated C-style string. It internally utilizes the standard library function std::strcat, passing the destination buffer and the C-string representation of the source String. The caller must ensure that the destination buffer has sufficient capacity to hold the combined string plus the null terminator to prevent buffer overflows. This utility facilitates seamless concatenation between custom String objects and legacy C-style strings, promoting compatibility and ease of use in mixed string handling scenarios.

params.dest: A pointer to the destination C-style string buffer where the contents of src will be appended. The buffer must be large enough to hold the resulting string.
params.src: A String object whose contents will be appended to the destination string.

char * strcat(char *dest, const String &.returns: A pointer to the destination string after concatenation.
char * strcat(char *dest, const String &.throws: This function does not throw exceptions; ensure the destination buffer has enough space to avoid undefined behavior.
char * strcat(char *dest, const String &.notes: The function assumes that the destination buffer is pre-allocated with sufficient space. Failure to do so may result in buffer overflows, leading to undefined behavior.
char * strcat(char *dest, const String &.rationale: Used in contexts where String objects need to be concatenated to existing C-style strings, such as in string manipulation routines or legacy code integrations, as demonstrated in functions like 'edit_split' which appends line break characters to split strings.
char * strcat(char *dest, const String &.name: strcat

Concatenates the string '

char * strncat(char *dest, const String.brief: Concatenates up to 'n' characters from a String object to a C-style string buffer.
char * strncat(char *dest, const String.details: This function enhances the standard 'strncat' by accepting a String object as the source. It internally retrieves the C-string representation of 'src' using 'src.c_str()' and appends up to 'n' characters to the 'dest' buffer. The operation assumes that 'dest' has sufficient allocated space to accommodate the new characters and the null terminator, preventing buffer overflows. The function returns the original 'dest' pointer, enabling chaining or direct use of the concatenated string. It relies on the caller to ensure that 'dest' is properly allocated and large enough for the operation.

params.dest: A pointer to the destination C-style string buffer where characters will be appended. The buffer must be large enough to hold the resulting string after concatenation.
params.n: The maximum number of characters to append from 'src' to 'dest'.
params.src: A String object from which characters are taken to append to 'dest'.

char * strncat(char *dest, const String.returns: A pointer to the 'dest' string after concatenation.
char * strncat(char *dest, const String.throws: This function does not throw exceptions; it relies on the caller ensuring that 'dest' has sufficient space to accommodate the appended characters and null terminator.
char * strncat(char *dest, const String.notes: The function performs no internal bounds checking; improper buffer size can lead to undefined behavior. It is designed for use in contexts where buffer sizes are managed externally.
char * strncat(char *dest, const String.rationale: This function is useful when concatenating a String object to a C-style string buffer in performance-critical code or legacy systems where direct buffer manipulation is common. It simplifies appending operations by accepting a String object directly, reducing the need for manual conversions.
char * strncat(char *dest, const String.name: strncat

char * strcpy(char *dest, const String &.brief: Copies the contents of a String object into a C-style null-terminated string buffer.
char * strcpy(char *dest, const String &.details: This inline function leverages std::strcpy to copy the character data from a String object into a specified character array. It retrieves the null-terminated C-string representation of the String via the c_str() method and copies it into the destination buffer. The caller must ensure that the destination buffer has enough space to accommodate the entire string plus the null terminator to avoid buffer overflows. This function is frequently used in contexts where legacy C-style string APIs, buffer manipulations, or raw character array processing are involved, such as copying arguments, message strings, or titles into buffers for further processing or output.

params.dest: Pointer to the destination character array where the string will be copied. Must be large enough to hold the source string and null terminator.
params.src: Reference to the String object whose content is to be copied.

char * strcpy(char *dest, const String &.throws: None; the function assumes the destination buffer has sufficient capacity. If the buffer is too small, buffer overflows may occur.
char * strcpy(char *dest, const String &.notes: Since the function relies on std::strcpy, it does not perform bounds checking. It is the caller's responsibility to ensure the destination buffer is adequately sized. This function simplifies copying String objects into C-style strings, facilitating interoperability with APIs or routines that require raw character arrays.
char * strcpy(char *dest, const String &.rationale: This function is used when a String object needs to be converted into a C-style string for legacy API calls, buffer manipulations, or string processing routines that operate on raw character arrays, as demonstrated in various parts of the codebase such as message formatting, argument handling, and title setting.
char * strcpy(char *dest, const String &.name: strcpy

char * strncpy(char *dest, const String.brief: Copies up to n characters from a String object to a C-style string buffer.
char * strncpy(char *dest, const String.details: This function copies up to n characters from the source String object into the specified destination buffer by invoking std::strncpy with the C-style string obtained from src.c_str(). It ensures that at most n characters are copied, including any null terminator if the source string is shorter than n. Since std::strncpy does not null-terminate if the source length exceeds n, the caller must ensure proper null termination if needed. This utility facilitates interoperability between the String class and legacy C APIs or low-level code that requires raw character arrays, providing a safe and convenient way to transfer string data.

params.dest: Pointer to the destination character array where the string will be copied.
params.n: Maximum number of characters to copy from src.
params.src: Reference to the String object whose content is to be copied.

char * strncpy(char *dest, const String.returns: Pointer to the destination buffer, dest.
char * strncpy(char *dest, const String.throws: This function does not throw exceptions; it relies on std::strncpy, which does not throw.
char * strncpy(char *dest, const String.notes: Since std::strncpy does not guarantee null-termination if the source string length exceeds n, it is recommended to manually null-terminate the destination buffer if necessary after copying.
char * strncpy(char *dest, const String.rationale: This function is used when copying data from the String class into a raw character buffer, especially when interfacing with C APIs or low-level system functions that require null-terminated strings or fixed-size buffers.
char * strncpy(char *dest, const String.name: strncpy

size_t strlen(const String &str).brief: Returns the length of the given String object in characters.
size_t strlen(const String &str).details: This inline function calculates the length of the provided String by calling its size() method, which returns the total number of characters contained within the string. Since the String class extends std::string and may include additional features, this function specifically relies on the standard size() method for efficiency and simplicity. It is frequently used in scenarios requiring size comparisons or buffer management, such as preventing overflow when concatenating strings, measuring string segments, or validating input lengths. Its inline nature ensures minimal overhead during repeated calls, making it suitable for performance-critical code segments.

params.str: The String object whose length is to be determined.

size_t strlen(const String &str).returns: The number of characters in the String as a size_t value.
size_t strlen(const String &str).throws: This function does not throw exceptions.
size_t strlen(const String &str).notes: As an inline function, it provides a fast, zero-overhead way to obtain string length, which is essential in performance-sensitive contexts such as buffer size checks and string manipulation routines.
size_t strlen(const String &str).rationale: Used in various parts of the codebase where string length information is needed to prevent buffer overflows, manage string concatenations, or process string data efficiently, as demonstrated by its usage in description handling, spell transformations, and buffer updates.
size_t strlen(const String &str).name: strlen

const char * strchr(const String &str, i.brief: Finds the first occurrence of a specified character within a String object.
const char * strchr(const String &str, i.details: This function wraps the standard C library function std::strchr by converting the custom String object to a C-style string using c_str(). It searches for the first occurrence of the character 'ch' within the string data. The returned pointer points directly into the internal character array of the String, or is nullptr if the character is not found. This allows efficient character searching within the String class, enabling tasks such as parsing, validation, and string analysis based on character positions.

params.ch: The character to locate within the string.
params.str: The String object in which to search for the character.

const char * strchr(const String &str, i.returns: A pointer to the first occurrence of the character in the string, or nullptr if not found.
const char * strchr(const String &str, i.throws: This function does not throw exceptions.
const char * strchr(const String &str, i.notes: Since the function returns a pointer into the internal string buffer, users should ensure the String object remains valid while using the returned pointer. The function provides a bridge between the custom String class and standard C string functions, facilitating compatibility and performance.
const char * strchr(const String &str, i.rationale: This function is useful when low-level character searches are needed within String objects, such as checking for special characters, delimiters, or control characters during parsing or validation routines.
const char * strchr(const String &str, i.name: strchr

int strcmp(const String &astr, const Str.brief: Performs a lexicographical comparison between two String objects.
int strcmp(const String &astr, const Str.details: This function compares two String instances by converting them to C-style strings using the c_str() method and then applying the standard library function std::strcmp. The comparison is case-sensitive and returns an integer indicating the lexical ordering: a negative value if the first string precedes the second, zero if they are equal, and a positive value if the first follows the second. It is typically used in scenarios requiring string sorting, searching, or conditional logic based on string ordering within the codebase. The String class extends std::string with additional features, but this function relies solely on the standard comparison behavior.

params.astr: The first String object to compare.
params.bstr: The second String object to compare.

int strcmp(const String &astr, const Str.returns: An integer indicating the lexical order: negative if astr < bstr, zero if equal, positive if astr > bstr.
int strcmp(const String &astr, const Str.throws: This function does not throw exceptions; it assumes that both String objects contain valid null-terminated strings.
int strcmp(const String &astr, const Str.notes: The comparison is case-sensitive. Since it uses std::strcmp, it performs a direct lexicographical comparison based on ASCII values.
int strcmp(const String &astr, const Str.rationale: Used in various parts of the codebase for comparing string identifiers, command keywords, or other textual data where lexicographical ordering is needed, such as sorting group names or parsing command arguments.
int strcmp(const String &astr, const Str.name: strcmp

int strncmp(const String &astr, const St.brief: Performs a case-sensitive comparison of up to n characters between two String objects.
int strncmp(const String &astr, const St.details: This function compares the first n characters of the provided String objects, 'astr' and 'bstr', by calling the standard library function std::strncmp on their underlying C-style strings obtained via c_str(). It is useful for prefix matching and lexicographical comparisons where only a specific number of characters are relevant. The comparison is case-sensitive, so differences in letter casing will influence the result. This wrapper ensures compatibility with the custom String class while leveraging the efficiency of the standard comparison function.

params.n: The maximum number of characters to compare.
params.astr: The first String object to be compared.
params.bstr: The second String object to be compared.

int strncmp(const String &astr, const St.returns: An integer indicating the lexical relationship between the compared substrings: less than zero if 'astr' is less, zero if they are equal, or greater than zero if 'astr' is greater.
int strncmp(const String &astr, const St.notes: The function performs a direct comparison of the underlying character arrays, making it suitable for performance-critical scenarios. It does not perform any null checks on the String objects, assuming they are valid and properly initialized.
int strncmp(const String &astr, const St.rationale: This function is used when precise, case-sensitive prefix comparisons are needed, such as in parsing JSON literals or matching command strings, where only a limited number of characters are relevant for decision-making.
int strncmp(const String &astr, const St.name: strncmp

int strcasecmp(const String &astr, const.brief: Performs a case-insensitive comparison between two String objects.
int strcasecmp(const String &astr, const.details: This function compares two String instances in a case-insensitive manner by converting each String to a C-style string using the c_str() method and then calling the standard strcasecmp function. It acts as a wrapper that facilitates case-insensitive lexical comparisons, which are essential in contexts such as user input matching, name comparisons, and social interaction handling within the application. By abstracting the conversion and comparison process, it simplifies code that requires case-insensitive logic, ensuring consistent behavior across various usage scenarios.

params.astr: The first String object to be compared.
params.bstr: The second String object to be compared.

int strcasecmp(const String &astr, const.returns: An integer indicating the lexical relationship: less than zero if astr is less than bstr, zero if they are equal, or greater than zero if astr is greater than bstr, ignoring case differences.
int strcasecmp(const String &astr, const.notes: This function relies on the standard strcasecmp function, which is POSIX-compliant and may not be available on all platforms. It assumes that the String class's c_str() method provides a null-terminated string compatible with strcasecmp.
int strcasecmp(const String &astr, const.rationale: Used in scenarios where case-insensitive string comparisons are necessary, such as matching user input, comparing names in social features, or searching within collections without regard to case differences. Its design ensures consistent and efficient case-insensitive comparisons across the codebase.
int strcasecmp(const String &astr, const.name: strcasecmp

int atoi(const String &astr).brief: Converts a custom String object to an integer value.
int atoi(const String &astr).details: This function extracts the underlying C-style null-terminated string from the provided String object using the c_str() method, then converts this string to an integer using the standard atoi function. It acts as a simple wrapper to enable direct conversion from the custom String class to an integer, which is useful in various parsing and numeric processing scenarios within the codebase. The implementation depends on the underlying atoi function for the actual conversion, ensuring compatibility with standard C++ string handling. This design allows seamless integration of the custom String class with existing numeric conversion routines.

params.astr: The String object containing the numeric string to be converted.

int atoi(const String &astr).returns: The integer value obtained from converting the string in 'astr'.
int atoi(const String &astr).notes: The function assumes that the String contains a valid numeric representation; behavior is undefined if the string does not represent a valid integer.
int atoi(const String &astr).rationale: This function is used whenever a String object needs to be converted to an integer, such as parsing user input, configuration data, or embedded numeric values within strings, facilitating easy and consistent conversions across the codebase.
int atoi(const String &astr).name: atoi
